title: Misreading Myanmar’s War: Why the Junta’s Recent Gains Don’t Mean Imminent Victory
gist: A China-Myanmar analyst argues the junta's 2025-2026 battlefield recovery is real but not decisive, driven by coalition disunity, arms shortages, and Chinese intervention, and that since the resistance still holds one-third to one-half of Myanmar the U.S. should keep backing it and refuse to recognize the junta despite rare-earth-access lobbying.

insights.claim: Myanmar's pro-democracy resistance wrested half the country from the Tatmadaw between 2021 and 2023 using drones and guerrilla ambushes, but that trajectory reversed in 2025 after the junta acquired an advanced drone fleet and conscripted tens of thousands.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Frames the war as a genuine but reversible swing, undercutting 'junta is winning' narratives that drive recognition debates.
insights.quote: the rebels wrested half the country from the Tatmadaw’s control.

insights.claim: By June 2026 the conflict is best understood as a new stalemate phase where neither side has a decisive advantage, with the resistance still controlling one-third to one-half of the country.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: The central judgment that should restrain premature U.S. diplomatic engagement with the junta.
insights.quote: the conflict is best understood as entering a new stalemate phase wherein neither side has achieved decisive advantage.
insights.locator: As of June 2026, the conflict is best understood

insights.claim: Coalition disunity hurts because the resistance's decentralized model of loosely coordinated 'cuts' is now less effective once the Tatmadaw has greater numbers and can isolate individual regions in a divide-and-conquer strategy.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Explains the structural reason for recent resistance losses and what a turnaround requires.
insights.quote: the resistance is fighting several smaller, disconnected conflicts across Myanmar instead of pursuing one unified strategy
insights.locator: In other words, the resistance is fighting several smaller

insights.claim: The resistance's arms supply chain (China-to-United Wa State Army-to-Three Brotherhood Alliance-to-People's Defense Forces) collapsed in mid-2025 when Beijing pressured the United Wa State Army to stop transferring weapons.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Shows the resistance's dependence on a Beijing-controlled black-market pipeline as a critical single point of failure.
insights.quote: China’s severing of the resistance’s arms supply chain dealt a decisive blow to resistance combat power.
insights.locator: This fragile arrangement collapsed in mid-2025.

insights.claim: China is the most instrumental factor because it amplifies the other two: Beijing shifted from hedging in 2021 to decisively backing the junta in 2024, forcing the Kokang and Ta'ang out of the war via blockades and ceasefires.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Positions Myanmar as an active arena of U.S.-China competition, raising the stakes of any U.S. recognition decision.
insights.quote: This is why China matters more than the other causes of the junta’s recovery.
insights.locator: In effect, China shifted from a more passive external actor

insights.claim: The resistance is consolidating: in March 2026 the National Unity Government and four aligned ethnic armed groups formed a Steering Council, with the Kachin Independence Army's participation a significant step given prior Chinese pressure.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Evidence the resistance can address its disunity weakness, supporting the 'not yet defeated' thesis.
insights.locator: The National Unity Government and four of the most closely aligned

insights.claim: China has limited leverage over ethnic armed groups based further from its border: it forced out the Kokang and Ta'ang but failed to subdue the Arakan Army or the Kachin.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Identifies the geographic limit of Chinese coercion, a structural opening for the resistance.
insights.quote: suggesting that China has limited leverage over groups based further from the international border.
insights.locator: Importantly, although Beijing forced the Kokang and Ta’ang

insights.claim: Controlling rare earth mines and Belt and Road projects is one of the few viable means for the resistance to counter Chinese pressure, as the Kachin have shown.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Links Myanmar's rare-earth geography to leverage in the U.S.-China contest, relevant to critical-minerals supply.
insights.quote: one of the only viable means of countering Chinese pressure is to gain control over rare earth mines and Belt and Road Initiative projects.
insights.locator: Regarding China, as the Kachin have recently shown

insights.claim: Engagement advocates citing access to rare earth minerals have found a receptive audience in the Trump administration amid an internal State Department policy review on recognizing the junta.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: Signals a live U.S. policy shift risk where mineral access could override democracy considerations.
insights.quote: citing prizes like access to rare earth minerals, have found a receptive audience in the Trump administration

insights.claim: Myers argues the U.S. should refuse to recognize Min Aung Hlaing's regime because recognition would relax sanctions, potentially provide billions in revenue, and reinforce a pro-China government in Southeast Asia.
insights.speaker: Lucas Myers
insights.so_what: The policy bottom line: maintaining sanctions denies junta revenue and is low-cost and bipartisan.
insights.quote: recognition would impart diplomatic legitimacy and begin the process of relaxing U.S. sanctions, potentially providing billions in denied revenue.
insights.locator: Instead, recognition would impart diplomatic legitimacy
